  // Helper function to read a cookie value
  function getCookie(name) {
    var value = "; " + document.cookie;
    var parts = value.split("; " + name + "=");
    if (parts.length == 2) return parts.pop().split(";").shift();
  }

  // Helper function to set a cookie
  function setCookie(name, value, days) {
    var expires = "";
    if (days) {
      var date = new Date();
      date.setTime(date.getTime() + (days*24*60*60*1000));
      expires = "; expires=" + date.toUTCString();
    }
    document.cookie = name + "=" + (value || "")  + expires + "; path=/";
  }

  // Function to update display eligibility based on the time gap
  function updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds) {
    setTimeout(function() {
      window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
      console.log('updateDisplayEligibility - OptinMonster is now display eligible');
    }, waitTimeSeconds * 1000); // Convert seconds to milliseconds
  }

  document.addEventListener('om.Campaign.show', function(event) {
    // console.log('om.Campaign.show', event.detail.Campaign);

    var now = new Date().getTime();
    setCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp', now, 1); // Store timestamp in cookie for 1 day
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';

    updateDisplayEligibility(minimumDisplayGap);
  });

  var now = new Date().getTime();
  var lastDisplayTime = parseInt(getCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp')) || 0;
  var timePassed = (now - lastDisplayTime) / 1000; // Convert milliseconds to seconds

  if (timePassed >= minimumDisplayGap) {
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
    console.log('OptinMonster display eligible on page load');
  } else {
    var waitTimeSeconds = minimumDisplayGap - timePassed;
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';
    updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds);
  }</script> <style id="uagb-style-conditional-extension">@media (min-width: 1025px){body .uag-hide-desktop.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-desktop{display:none !important}}@media (min-width: 768px) and (max-width: 1024px){body .uag-hide-tab.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-tab{display:none !important}}@media (max-width: 767px){body .uag-hide-mob.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-mob{display:none !important}}</style><style id="uagb-style-frontend-62682">.uagb-heading-text mark {

is-type-video is-provider-youtube wp-embed-aspect-16-9 wp-has-aspect-ratio"><div class="lyte-wrapper" style="width:1280px;max-width:100%;margin:5px auto;"><div class="lyte hidef lP" id="WYL_YdH9FPALx2U"><div id="lyte_YdH9FPALx2U" data-src="//i.ytimg.com/vi/YdH9FPALx2U/maxresdefault.jpg" class="pL"><div class="tC"><div class="tT"></div></div><div class="play"></div><div class="ctrl"><div class="Lctrl"></div><div class="Rctrl"></div></div></div><noscript><a href="https://youtu.be/YdH9FPALx2U" rel="nofollow"><img decoding="async" src="https://i.ytimg.com/vi/YdH9FPALx2U/0.jpg" alt="YouTube video thumbnail" width="1280" height="700" /><br />Watch this video on YouTube</a></noscript></div></div><div class="lL" style="max-width:100%;width:1280px;margin:5px auto;"></div><figcaption></figcaption></figure><div style="height:40px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>If you are putting in a serious amount of effort with your training and nutrition, you owe it to yourself to take the ten extra minutes each week to track your progress seriously.</p><p>I’d go as far as to say it’s the biggest differentiator between those that are successful and those that aren’t.</p><p class="has-text-align-left">Why?</p><p class="has-text-align-left">Because without proper tracking data, you can’t gauge whether or not you are progressing as hoped. You won’t have objective data points from which to base your decisions on when you stall in some area, and there is a good chance that you will get stuck spinning your wheels, not knowing what to tweak to get yourself back on track.</p><p class="has-text-align-left">Perhaps you’ve already experienced this frustration?</p><p class="has-text-align-left"><strong>Consider the following:</strong></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>A lack of weight change does not necessarily mean that you haven’t lost body fat.</li><li>A weight increase doesn’t necessarily mean that you’ve gained body fat.</li><li><a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-bulk/">Weight gain when bulking won’t be from muscle alone</a>.</li><li>A lack of training progress doesn’t necessarily mean that a training plan is to blame.</li><li><a href="/is-it-better-to-forget-about-body-fat-percentage">Body fat measurement methods <em>all</em> have accuracy issues</a>, so they can’t be relied upon to gauge progress in the short term.</li></ul><p class="has-text-align-left">If the way you’re currently tracking isn’t sufficient to tease out the differences, then you need to improve it. Fortunately, this article is here to help. It will guide you through the art of proper progress tracking that I’ve developed over the last 10 years working with clients online. It is easy to understand, quick to implement, and I’ve included a spreadsheet tracker you can download also.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="📙" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f4d9.svg"> What follows is a sample chapter from my book,<strong> <a href="https://rippedbody.com/adjustments-manual/">The Diet Adjustments Manual</a></strong>.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center"><span style="font-size: 1em;">The three body progress tracking methods to avoid</span></h2><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="404" height="380" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Dont-try-to-gauge-progress-based-on-body-fat-measurement-devices.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-59662"></figure><p><strong>1. Don’t try to assess your progress by a body-fat percentage estimation tool. </strong>All methods have inaccuracies and inconsistencies. I’ve talked about this <a href="https://rippedbody.com/is-it-better-to-forget-about-body-fat-percentage/">here</a> and <a href="https://rippedbody.com/podcast-james-krieger/">here</a>. If you wish to get an <em>estimation</em>, use my <a href="https://rippedbody.com/body-fat-guide/">body-fat percentage pictures</a> and the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-calculate-body-fat-percentage/">US Navy Body Fat Calculator</a>. Over longer time frames, these are sufficient. In the shorter time frames where all the decisions need to happen, they are woefully inadequate and will more than likely just leave you in the shit.</p><p><strong>2. Do not try to use an activity tracker to estimate your calorie burn and adjust your diet each day to the numbers it gives. </strong>These devices are also notoriously inaccurate. The activity multiplier in your TDEE calculation took care of your activity levels. Yes, they will vary a little from day to day, but this is a small part of a much <a href="https://rippedbody.com/coaching-lessons-dont-miss-obvious/" data-wpil-monitor-id="57">bigger picture</a>, so you don’t need to worry about it.</p><p><strong>3. Do not try to gauge progress by how you look in the mirror. </strong>The brain plays tricks on us. How we see ourselves changes due to a phenomenon known as <em>perceptual adaptation</em>. Oh, and how defined we look can change from day to day also.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">The 8 Ways I Get Clients to Measure and Chart Body Progress</h2><p>There are eight key ways I now get clients to track progress. The data points taken together will help you <a href="https://rippedbody.com/as-you-cut/">navigate the fluctuations in weight</a> and determine whether you need to adjust or just keep doing what you’re doing.</p><p class="has-text-align-left">This is how your data will look if you track as per this guide:</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="356" height="644" src="/wp-content/uploads/2016/10/Tracking-Log-Example.png" alt="Body Measurements - How To Track Progress" class="wp-image-44352" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/10/Tracking-Log-Example.png 356w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/10/Tracking-Log-Example-249x450.png 249w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 356px) 100vw, 356px"></figure><p class="has-text-align-center"><span style="color: #ff0000;"></span></p><p class="has-text-align-center"><strong><a href="https://rippedbody.com/tracker" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">You can download a copy of the progress tracker spreadsheet immediately for free here.</a></strong><a href="https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1OVazxxpAbhgw5itjND8fM3Z-uVGS0ixbYfsl8QS-YIA/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"><br></a>(<img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg"> Click <em>File</em> in the top left, then <em>Download</em>.)</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading">1. Weigh Yourself Daily</h3><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg"> <strong>Weigh yourself every morning, upon waking, after going to the toilet. Note the weekly average.</strong></p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="589" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking_Tools_-_Scale_Weight-1024x589.png" alt="" class="wp-image-60461" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking_Tools_-_Scale_Weight-1024x589.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking_Tools_-_Scale_Weight-450x259.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking_Tools_-_Scale_Weight.png 1113w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>You can choose to do this at night, but most people will find a morning habit easier to stay consistent with.</p><p>Put the scale, notepad, and pen next to the toilet so you can’t forget. Note each day to the nearest 0.1 and note the weekly average to the nearest 0.1 in your progress tracker.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="430" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-log-weight-data-1024x430.jpg" alt="Weight tracker - how to log your data" class="wp-image-60462" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-log-weight-data-1024x430.jpg 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-log-weight-data-450x189.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-log-weight-data.jpg 1228w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>WHY I RECOMMEND DAILY WEIGHING</strong></h4><p>As explained in my article on <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-adjust-macros/">macro adjustments when dieting</a>, your weight will fluctuate. Weighing ourselves daily, noting the average at the end of each week, and then comparing weeks helps to smooth out these fluctuations in the data and make interpretation easier.</p><p>As a reminder, here are all the reasons that <a href="https://rippedbody.com/holiday-weight-gain/">weight fluctuations happen</a>, aside from fat and muscle mass changes:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Water due to hydration status (perspiration and respiration).</li><li>Water due to a change in salt intake.</li><li>Water (retention) due to stress or the menstrual cycle.</li><li>Glycogen due to a change in carb intake.</li><li>Bowel content, because some foods have a higher ‘gut residue’ (they stay in the gut for longer).</li></ul><p>Some clients have found daily weighing stressful due to the fluctuations. But I’ve found that when I’ve taken the time to explain the causes and show them several data sets of past clients, it has helped immensely.</p><p>So, if you find the idea of daily weighing stressful, the reasons presented here and the data you’ll see later in the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/books/" data-wpil-monitor-id="58">book</a> should help.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="measurements">2. Take Weekly Body Measurements</h3><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg"> <strong>Measure your body circumference in nine places, once per week, to the nearest 0.1 cm.</strong></p><p>I suggest you measure in the morning when you wake up, after going to the toilet. I get clients to do this on a Saturday.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="1024" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-measure-your-progress-1024x1024.png" alt="How To Track Body Measurements" class="wp-image-60463" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-measure-your-progress-1024x1024.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-measure-your-progress-450x450.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-measure-your-progress-150x150.png 150w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-measure-your-progress.png 1080w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="430" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-log-measurement-data-1024x430.jpg" alt="How To Log Body Measurements" class="wp-image-60464" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-log-measurement-data-1024x430.jpg 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-log-measurement-data-450x189.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-log-measurement-data.jpg 1228w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>To help you get consistent measurements:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>Do it yourself </strong>rather than relying on a partner, as you are the only person that will always be with you.</li><li><strong>Get an automatically tightening tape </strong>(commonly branded as an Orbitape or Myotape). This will make self-measuring easier, and the tightness of the tape will be consistent.</li><li><strong>For the chest, </strong>measure at the nipple-line, being sure not to get the tape at an angle or twisted behind your back. Take a deep breath and hold it. Don’t flex your lats or chest unless you have experience controlling how much you can make the lats flare.</li><li><strong>For the legs, </strong>stand, tense, and measure at the widest point.</li><li><strong>For the arms, </strong>curl your biceps, tense, and measure at the widest point.</li><li><strong>For the stomach, </strong>tense and measure at the navel, three finger-widths above, and three finger-widths below.</li></ul><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="422" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking-Tools-Tape-Measure-for-the-Body-1024x422.png" alt="Tracking Tools - Tape Measure for the Body" class="wp-image-60465" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking-Tools-Tape-Measure-for-the-Body-1024x422.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking-Tools-Tape-Measure-for-the-Body-450x185.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking-Tools-Tape-Measure-for-the-Body-1536x633.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking-Tools-Tape-Measure-for-the-Body-1568x646.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Tracking-Tools-Tape-Measure-for-the-Body.png 1754w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption"><span style="color:#000000" class="tadv-color">What a self-tightening tape looks like.</span></figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>WHY I RECOMMEND TAKING WEEKLY BODY MEASUREMENTS</strong></h4><p>When combined with the scale weight, body measurements help us to gauge muscle growth and fat loss in different areas.</p><p>The 0.1 cm degree of accuracy is useful for noting small changes and trends in the data. It also sets clients up with a mindset on precision, reminding them that they need to take the data seriously. Without reliable data, as an <a href="https://rippedbody.com/coaching/" data-wpil-monitor-id="28">online coach</a>, I am blind. You will be also.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading">3. Take Monthly Photos</h3><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg"> <strong>Take two photos, front and side, once every four weeks.</strong></p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="885" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Monthly-Photo-Example-Klint-crop-1024x885.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-60466" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Monthly-Photo-Example-Klint-crop-1024x885.jpg 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Monthly-Photo-Example-Klint-crop-450x389.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Monthly-Photo-Example-Klint-crop.jpg 1080w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>Use the same lighting conditions, camera, camera angle, time of day, and pose. Do this yourself rather than relying on someone to take them.</p><p>Resist the urge to forcibly stick your stomach out in your first set of photos. The goal with the photos is not to have the most striking before-after shots but to have a reliable visual gauge of progress.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>WHY I RECOMMEND TAKING PHOTOS</strong></h4><p>Being able to see changes in definition month to month can be very useful for motivation. I’ve experimented with weekly and fortnightly photos with clients, and I’m convinced that every four weeks is best as the changes are often too small to be noticeable at higher frequencies.</p><p>Competitors should consider adding a third picture from the back, as this can show changes in the lower back, hip, and ass fat which the front and side photos will not towards the end of the diet.</p><p>I prefer relying on data for decision-making purposes, but three exceptions come to mind where I find photos to be more useful:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li>When I estimate initial body fat.</li><li>When I gauge whether a competitor is <a href="https://rippedbody.com/lean-enough-to-bulk/" data-wpil-monitor-id="59">lean enough</a>.</li><li>When I help people decide when to <a href="https://rippedbody.com/dont-recalculate/" data-wpil-monitor-id="60">transition from a cut to a bulk</a>.</li></ol><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading">4. Rate Weekly Dietary Adherence</h3><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg"> <strong>Note the adherence to your weekly calorie target as a percentage of your totals for the week.</strong></p><p>So, if you go 15% over your calorie targets, write 115%. If you go 10% under your targets, write 90%.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="288" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-weekly-dietary-adherence-1024x288.jpeg" alt="How To Rate Weekly Diet Adherence" class="wp-image-60467" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-weekly-dietary-adherence-1024x288.jpeg 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-weekly-dietary-adherence-450x127.jpeg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-weekly-dietary-adherence.jpeg 1200w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>The way to find your daily calorie targets is to multiply the calorie values of each macronutrient and then add them together.</p><p>For protein and carbs, this is 4 calories per gram; for fats, this is 9 calories per gram. Thus, if your <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macros/" data-wpil-monitor-id="259">macros</a> are P:200, C:200, F:50, your calorie target is 2050 daily, 14,350 weekly.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg">  If you eat 16,000 kcal, your adherence total is 111%. (16000/14350*100)</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg">  If you eat 12,000, your adherence total is 84%. (12000/14350*100)</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>WHY I RECOMMEND LOGGING DIETARY ADHERENCE AS A PERCENTAGE OF CALORIE TOTALS</strong></h4><p>You aren’t going to be perfect. Your memory is fallible and has a recency bias.</p><p>When looking at your data to determine <a href="https://rippedbody.com/why-diet-adjustments-are-needed/">whether your macros need to be adjusted</a>, you need to know how well you adhered each week.</p><p>Your macro targets might be perfectly fine, but the issue is that you haven’t been adhering to them, and without the data it’s easy to forget.</p><p>Also, by tracking things as a percentage of calorie totals hit each week, you get the opportunity to compensate for any mistakes made earlier in the week on later days. You don’t want to do this too often, or you will compromise recovery.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg"> <strong>Don’t make the mistake of writing the percentage of meals you adhered to.</strong></p><p>Let’s say you eat three meals per day and have two meals off plan in a week, calling them “free meals.” This would give you a 90% meal adherence total, which on the face of it, could be considered good adherence.</p><p>However, it’s conceivable that with a starter, large main course, and dessert, you could put down 2500 calories in each of these meals.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg">  Assuming a 2100 calorie daily target and perfect adherence to your otherwise 700 calorie meals, this would give you a 3600 kcal surplus for the week, which is an adherence total of 124%. (2100*7 + 3600)/2100*7</p><p>This would wipe out your calorie deficit entirely, but if you were to log 90% instead of 124%, you’d be forgiven for thinking that the lack of progress wasn’t due to poor adherence but the macros being too high.</p><p>If you cut macros, adherence is harder. If you manage to sustain it, you’re likely to then consume even more in your two <em>free meals </em>(typically at the weekend), and the cycle of misery will continue.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading">5. Rate Weekly Training Adherence</h3><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg"> <strong>Note your training adherence as a percentage of workouts completed.</strong></p><p>So, if you have four workouts planned for the week and you only did three of them, rate your adherence as 75%.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="288" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-weekly-training-adherence-1024x288.jpeg" alt="How To Rate Weekly Training Adherence" class="wp-image-60468" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-weekly-training-adherence-1024x288.jpeg 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-weekly-training-adherence-450x127.jpeg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-weekly-training-adherence.jpeg 1200w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>Fluctuations in performance are normal and to be expected. This percentage is not a reflection of how well you feel you performed or whether you progressed, only whether you turned up and put in the work.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>WHY I RECOMMEND RATING WEEKLY TRAINING ADHERENCE</strong></h4><p>If you haven’t been sticking to your training plan, then you can’t expect to progress with it. However, without the data, it’s sometimes easy to miss the fact that you haven’t been faithfully following the program well enough to gauge the efficacy.</p><p>If this number is consistently below ~85% (meaning you’re missing one in six sessions), then you need to re-prioritize training in your schedule. If that is not possible, <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-build-training-programs/">re-organize the training volume into fewer training days</a>.</p><p>Yes, this will mean longer, more tiring sessions with a potential performance drop off toward the end. But this is preferable to consistently missing workouts.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading">6. Rate Sleep Quality, Stress, Hunger, and Fatigue</h3><p><strong><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg">  Rate all of these on a 0–5 scale.</strong></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li><b>Sleep issues? </b>(0 = no issues, high-quality sleep. 5 = insomnia.)</li><li><b>Stress levels</b> (0 = no stress, 5 = divorce or a death in the family.)</li><li><b>Hunger issues?</b> (0 = no issues, 5 = extreme hunger.)</li><li><b>Fatigue/lethargy? </b>(0 = no issues, 5 = exceptionally fatigued.)</li></ul><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="288" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-sleep-stress-hunger-fatigue-1024x288.jpeg" alt="How To Rate Sleep, Stress, Hunger and Fatigue" class="wp-image-60469" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-sleep-stress-hunger-fatigue-1024x288.jpeg 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-sleep-stress-hunger-fatigue-450x127.jpeg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-to-rate-sleep-stress-hunger-fatigue.jpeg 1200w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>WHY I RECOMMEND TRACKING SUBJECTIVE FEELINGS OF SLEEP QUALITY, STRESS, HUNGER, AND FATIGUE</strong></h4><p>It’s normal to have some issues as you progress. Knowing about these things will help tell you when you can be more aggressive or when you need to back off a little.</p><p><strong>Sleep quality </strong>will affect training performance and recovery, and therefore muscle retention when in a calorie<br>deficit. Sleep quality affects hunger and energy levels. So, if you are hungry, your training has been shitty recently, or you’ve been feeling lethargic, but you see that your sleep quality has been low, fixing it is the most likely cure.</p><p><a href="https://rippedbody.com/stress/" data-wpil-monitor-id="61">Stress will negatively impact training</a> performance as well as recovery and can cause water retention. Stress can also affect sleep. So, if your weight hasn’t been coming down in the last few weeks, but your stress levels are exceptionally high, then water retention masking fat losses may be to blame.</p><p><strong>Chronic hunger </strong>can signify that the <a href="/calories">caloric deficit is too high</a>. However, high stress levels or poor food choices can sometimes cause this. So, if you are hungry and stressed, the one may be causing the other, and you need to work on the root cause of the stress.</p><p><strong>Energy levels </strong>affect workout performance. This, in turn, affects our ability to maintain muscle when dieting or grow it when bulking. If energy levels are low, it could be a sign that you need to raise caloric intake. However, it could also be due to poor sleep or high stress, so consider these things before increasing calorie intake.</p><p>As you can see, there are many interactions to consider. I’ll simplify the decision-making process with the flowcharts in the later chapters on mid-diet adjustments.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading">7. Keep summary notes on your key lifts</h3><p>List each of the main compound lifts in your training plan in the ‘Key Lift’ section beneath the rest of the data. Every two weeks, write one of the following three things:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>‘Progressing, recovered.’</li><li>‘Not progressing, recovered.’</li><li>‘Not progressing, not recovered.’</li></ul><p>‘Not recovered’ in this sense means your performance is affected after a warm-up (the weights feel heavier than usual for that exercise). This shouldn’t be considered good or bad, so don’t attach a meaning to it. It is normal for this to happen in waves across our training week(s) as fatigue builds and dissipates with training the training cycle and as stresses come and go.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>WHY I RECOMMEND YOU KEEP SUMMARY NOTES ABOUT TRAINING PROGRESSION</strong></h4><p>Keeping summary data will help you to see the connections between macro adjustments, sleep, stress, hunger, and fatigue, without being overwhelmed by the full training log.</p><p>Suppose you see that you aren’t progressing as well as expected in certain areas, despite sleep, stress, and adherence being on point for a couple of consecutive periods. In that case, you know you need to take a deload, tweak something in your <a href="https://rippedbody.com/training-program-choice/" data-wpil-monitor-id="62">training program</a>, or perhaps raise your caloric intake.</p><p>If you are not progressing but feeling recovered, it could be a sign you need to increase training volume. Please refer to the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/training/">training articles</a> or <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="📘" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f4d8.svg"> <a href="https://muscleandstrengthpyramids.com/">The Muscle and Strength Pyramid: Training</a>.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading">8. Keep a detailed training log</h3><p>Either in a notebook or a separate tab of your tracking spreadsheet, keep a full training log. Note the <strong><em>sets </em></strong><strong>* </strong><strong><em>reps </em></strong><strong>* </strong><strong><em>load </em></strong>(including the weight of the bar.</p><p>For example, if you performed 3 sets of 12 reps at 35 kg, write <strong><em>3*12*35</em></strong>. Here is an example:</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="990" height="554" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Example-Training-Log-for-Adjustments-Manual-2020.png" alt="How to log your training program and progress" class="wp-image-60470" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Example-Training-Log-for-Adjustments-Manual-2020.png 990w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Example-Training-Log-for-Adjustments-Manual-2020-450x252.png 450w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 990px) 100vw, 990px"></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>If you lower the load or do fewer reps on subsequent sets, note it like this: <strong><em>2*12*40, 12*35</em></strong>, or <strong><em>2*12*40, 10*40</em></strong>. Avoid clutter, don’t note your warm-up sets nor the units.</p><p>If you’re not familiar with the rating of perceived exertion (RPE) notation, it is a method to help match the load used each day to your readiness. It’s explained <a href="https://rippedbody.com/rpe/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">here</a>.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>WHY I RECOMMEND YOU KEEP A TRAINING LOG</strong></h4><p>There are two primary reasons:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li>This allows you to dig into the details of your program when you have determined that you need to make a change.</li><li>You need a record of what you lifted the week before so that you can choose what you lift this week. I keep a screenshot of my training program on my phone. I put the phone on airplane mode. This serves the dual function of making sure I’m not disturbed when lifting something heavy and keeping me away from social media distractions.</li></ol><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="800" height="800" src="/wp-content/uploads/2016/12/TRACKING-PROGRESS-IS-ESSENTIAL.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-49559" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/12/TRACKING-PROGRESS-IS-ESSENTIAL.jpg 800w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/12/TRACKING-PROGRESS-IS-ESSENTIAL-150x150.jpg 150w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/12/TRACKING-PROGRESS-IS-ESSENTIAL-450x450.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/12/TRACKING-PROGRESS-IS-ESSENTIAL-768x768.jpg 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/12/TRACKING-PROGRESS-IS-ESSENTIAL-150x150@2x.jpg 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 800px) 100vw, 800px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption"><span class="tadv-color" style="color:#000000"><em>For more useful stuff, check out my <a href="https://www.instagram.com/andy_rippedbody/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Instagram</a>.</em></span></figcaption></figure><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">Warning: More Fitness Tracking data is not always better</h2><p class="has-text-align-left">Looking at data is something I do half of my working week. How I get clients to present it to me is something I have thought <em>very</em> long and hard about. Think carefully before adding other data, as it can overwhelm you with detail.</p><p class="has-text-align-left">That said, the one additional item I would suggest you track is <em>motivation</em>, rated weekly on a scale of 1-10. This is something I have clients write in their email updates along with any questions or concerns because I want them to explain the reason they feel this way. Also, as a coach, the text dialogue at the update points can provide invaluable insights.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><a href="https://rippedbody.com/adjustments-manual/"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="634" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-1024x634.png" alt="The Diet Adjustments Manual by Andy Morgan - Device Display" class="wp-image-60161" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-1024x634.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-450x278.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-1536x950.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-1568x970.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display.png 2041w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></a></figure><p>Thank you for reading. To learn how to interpret this tracking data and making adjustments to keep yourself progressing, check out my book, <a href="https://rippedbody.com/adjustments-manual/"><strong>The Diet Adjustments Manual</strong>.</a></p><p>Questions welcomed in the comments. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🙏🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f64f-1f3fb.svg"><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="❤️" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/2764.svg"></p><p>– Andy</p></div><form action="https://app.kit.com/forms/7749683/subscriptions" style="background-color:#fffefa;border-radius:10px" class="seva-form formkit-form" method="post" data-sv-form="7749683" data-uid="8462769b72" data-format="inline" data-version="5" min-width="400 500 600 700"><div data-style="full" style="--bg-border-radius:5px"><div data-element="column" class="formkit-column"><div class="formkit-header" style="color:#333333;font-weight:700" data-element="header"><h2><strong>STOP GUESSING. itemprop="name">Andy Morgan</h4> </a><div class="post-author-desc">Hi! The Ripped Body® Method is an evidence-based, data-driven approach to physique transformation.
⠀
For the majority of my 20s, I fell for the scams of the fitness industry. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🤦🏻‍♂️" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f926-1f3fb-200d-2642-fe0f.svg">
⠀
This site is my revenge. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="😬" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f62c.svg">
⠀
Since 2011, I’ve written guides explaining how we get results to help readers avoid the same path of frustration. We’ve helped over 2,000 busy working guys transform their bodies WITHOUT supplements, fad diets, or the process dominating their lives.
⠀
<img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="📩" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f4e9.svg"> Enter your email address in the box at the bottom of this page and I'd be honored to show you how.</div></div></div></div></div><div class="wpdiscuz_top_clearing"></div><div id="comments" class="comments-area"><div id="respond" style="width: 0;height: 0;clear: both;margin: 0;padding: 0;"></div><div id="wpdcom" class="wpdiscuz_unauth wpd-default wpd-layout-1 wpd-comments-open"><div class="wc_social_plugin_wrapper"></div><div class="wpd-form-wrap"><div class="wpd-form-head"><div class="wpd-auth"><div class="wpd-login"></div></div></div><div class="wpd-form wpd-form-wrapper wpd-main-form-wrapper" id="wpd-main-form-wrapper-0_0"><form method="post" enctype="multipart/form-data" data-uploading="false" class="wpd_comm_form wpd_main_comm_form"><p style="font-size:20px; font-weight:900; text-transform:uppercase; margin-bottom:10px; margin-top:40px;">Got a question?     <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Andy,</p>
<p>If body fat % can’t be measured accurately and shouldn’t be used to track progress, how should a beginner practically decide when to end their first cut? You mention ~10% body fat, but without reliable measurement, what signs should we use?</p>
<p>Also, is aiming that low realistic or appropriate for a new trainee (3 months lifting), or should the first cut end earlier? (I’ve seen sources suggest ending at ~23 BMI and starting a bulking then).</p>
<p>Thanks!</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hey Andy,</p>
<p>I’m a fan of your page and how simply and accurately you explain bodybuilding and everything related to it. I’ve gone through your resources, and they have greatly helped me.     <div class="wpd-reply-to">

    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-89721">
        Kaushik Avancha
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Kaushik, thank you for the question.</p>
<p>When we bulk, we gain fat along with muscle. This is unavoidable. The problem is that starting at 15–20% body fat, you don’t have a lot of runway to bulk before you feel “too fat” and want to cut. This means you will likely end the bulk before you have gained a meaningful amount of muscle, and when you cut down again, you’ll be disappointed, possibly not noticing much difference, putting you back where you started.</p>
<p>There are two ways out of this common trap; both require accepting temporary discomfort: bulk for long enough and accept that you’ll feel a little too fat for a while, or cut and embrace feeling “too skinny” for a while.</p>
<p>Most people can’t accept and aren’t willing to do this, so they stay in physique purgatory. The fitness industry is partly to blame, as it sells false promises, a way out of this trap without the discomfort. Ignore this nonsense.</p>
<p>You’ll find my article on this here: <a href="https://rippedbody.com/cut-or-bulk/" rel="ugc">Should I Bulk vs Cut (or Recomp)? The Definitive Guide</a></p>

</div>

    <p>Hi Chris, thank you for the question.</p>
<p>As a rough heuristic, 2–2.5 cm off the stomach in two or more places is roughly 4–5 pounds of fat loss. </p>
<p>This one pops out to me, but nothing else obviously works well enough, most of the time, to publish as a recommendation.</p>
<p>I’ll have to run some statistical analysis on client data at some point to see.</p>

    <p>Hi Andy, I’m wondering how to evaluate whether the changes in body measurements for a client from week to week are considered satisfactory.     </a>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Abdulla, thank you for the questions.</p>
<p>1. I’ve observed that a 2-2.5 cm drop in measurements from two or more places on the stomach roughly correlates with 4–5 pounds of fat loss. Bear in mind my clients are male.</p>
<p>2. Yes, measurements fluctuate. For this reason, I set no “expectation” for how they will change from week to week, nor try to interpret that. I only look at the trends over four weeks as a minimum (just like with body weight). This is mainly due to slight inconsistencies in how people measure, but also because of differences in how “pumped” people are, and the gut content, water, and glycogen swings.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hey Andy, </p>
<p>This year I did a 8 week cut and lost 15 lbs & got to about 12-13% body fat. But I stopped and went back to maintenance and have binged many days. Since ending my cut 3 months ago, I’ve gained 7 lbs since my lowest weigh in. I’ve been tracking calories relentlessly for a year now. MacroFactor App says my metabolism has dropped down to 2600 calories (I’m 6’0” and 205 lbs). That means that I’d have to eat about 2,000 calories or even lower to get to my goal weight (190). Is that healthy? Or should I stick to maintenance and just cut later this year?</p>

</div>

    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/diet-progress-tracking/#comment-84921"></i></span>
</div>
</div>
    
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Andy:</p>
<p>A quick question about arm and leg measurements.</p>
<p>When trying to lose fat, it's intuitive that I want my belly measurements to go down. And it's pretty intuitive that I want my chest measurements to also go down, given that most of us carry extra fat around the back and chest.</p>
<p>But for arms, and to a lesser extent legs, especially if I'm already pretty thin there, do I want to see them go down, go up, or stay the same? Would decreasing limb girths necessarily indicate that I am losing fat there also, or could some of those reductions perhaps be unwanted muscle loss?</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-reply-button">

    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Well, you *want* to see them go up, but most clients find these go down, even if all lifts go up and therefore, we're confident of muscle gain.</p>
<p>There's fat under the skin and within the muscle, and this is usually lost faster than muscle can be grown. There are a couple of visuals on this here: <a href="https://rippedbody.com/cut-or-bulk/" rel="ugc">https://rippedbody.com/cut-or-bulk/</a></p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Andy – the waist measurement confuses me. “<strong>For the stomach, </strong>tense…”  When I let my belly out – just relax it but not push it out – it is 46″!!!     </a>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Tense means to flex the muscles, in this case, the abs. This is different to relaxing or sucking the stomach in. If this is difficult, please choose any position where you can get a consistent measurement.</p>

</div>

</div><div id="wpdiscuz_form_anchor-84123_0"></div><div id="wpd-comm-84125_84123" class="comment byuser comment-author-morganandrew bypostauthor even depth-2 wpd-comment wpd-reply wpd_comment_level-2"><div class="wpd-comment-wrap wpd-blog-user wpd-blog-administrator wpd-blog-user wpd-blog-post_author">

    <div id="comment-84125" class="wpd-comment-right">
    <div class="wpd-comment-header">
    <div class="wpd-comment-author ">
    Andy Morgan
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="August 10, 2022 10:04">
    <i class="far fa-clock" aria-hidden="true"></i>
    August 10, 2022 10:04
</div>
    
    
    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/diet-progress-tracking/#comment-84125"></i></span>
</div>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-reply-to">
    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-84123">
        Mark
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Mark, thank you for the question.</p>
<p>So, you need to eat more but not count calories. The problem is that when you count, it causes you stress. And when you don’t count, your hunger and fullness cues cause you to maintain your current weight. Does this summarize the situation well?</p>
<p>This latter point is how the body is supposed to work.</p>
<p>Remember, we’re in a fitness bubble. Most people don’t count calories, yet, year on year, they maintain their weight within a couple of pounds, obviously with this trend slightly upwards. That’s pretty incredible and speaks to the power of our hunger and fullness cues to regulate body weight.</p>
<p>Hence, when <a href="https://rippedbody.com/before-you-count/">commonsense, habit-based interventions</a> don’t work for dieting (drink less alcohol, walk more, eat more vegetables and lean protein), I typically recommend people count calories.</p>
<p>Now, you have the opposite problem and struggle to <em>gain</em> weight. I’m not going to sit here with a straight face and tell you that the solution is to drink more alcohol, walk less, and eat fewer vegetables and protein because that would be harmful to your health.</p>
<p>And I’m not going to say “just eat more” because you’ve obviously tried that, and your hunger and fullness queues keep bringing you back.</p>
<p>There are three strategies to consider:</p>
<p>1. Meal frequency<br>
2. Meal repetition<br>
3. Food choices</p>
<p><strong>Meal Frequency</strong><br>
We know that limiting the number of meals we eat helps many people lose weight. The opposite is also true. So if you have been limiting yourself to two meals a day, lunch and dinner, for example, consider adding breakfast. If you’ve been eating three, try four. And try to space your meals out evenly throughout the day.</p>
<p><strong>Meal Repetition</strong><br>
If you don’t eat out a lot and find that you default to have the same 6–8 meals on rotation, you can do something simple like add in a “fist” of carbs to one of your meals each day and see if this starts to tick your weight up. The key is not to change your other meals because you feel full.</p>
<p>This isn’t likely to be successful unless you modify your food choices.</p>
<p><strong>Food Choices</strong><br>
I recommend allowing yourself to eat more of the foods you’re currently labeling as “bad” more often. </p>
<p>So, eat more calorie-dense, hyper-palatable foods. — Think of swapping cereal for some of your regular carb intake, add some fruit juices or protein shakes, perhaps a little more chocolate, for example.</p>
<p>This will allow you to consume more calories each day without thinking about it. It doesn’t have to be extreme; 200-300 calories above what you’re currently eating should do it.</p>
<p>Remember, life isn’t a game of Mario. Once we’ve met our micronutrient needs, we don’t get bonus health points for having more — we just pee or poop them out.</p>
<p>Try not to think of your diet in terms of what foods we should <em>exclude</em>, but rather, what foods we should <em>include</em> first. And as long as you maintain a diet rich in lean meats, fruits, and vegetables, there’s no harm in including the so-called “empty calories” from so-called “bad” foods. — In this context, struggling to gain weight, they are good for you and your goals.</p>

</div>

    <p>Glad that was useful, Mark.</p>
<p>It’s not my place to critique specific food choices, so I’ve deleted what you wrote there. What matters is the overall context of your diet. Download my Nutrition Setup Guide (see the box at the end of the article) as that answers your questions.</p>
<p>Here’s my article on <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-bulk/">How To Bulk Without Getting Fat</a>. </p>

  var bodyClassList = document.body.classList;
  var optinMonsterSegment = 'generic'; // Default

  // Slug arrays
  var trainingSegmentSlugs = ['training']; // Add more training slugs as needed
  var nutritionSegmentSlugs = ['nutrition']; // Add more nutrition slugs as needed

  // Function to check if any slug exists in body class
  function segmentSlugExists(slugArray) {
    return slugArray.some(function(slug) {
      return bodyClassList.contains('category-' + slug);
    });
  }

  // Check for training or nutrition segments
  if (segmentSlugExists(trainingSegmentSlugs)) {
    optinMonsterSegment = 'training';
  } else if (segmentSlugExists(nutritionSegmentSlugs)) {
    optinMonsterSegment = 'nutrition';
  }

metadata.author: Andy Morgan
metadata.description: A full guide to tracking your diet and training progress, for serious, physique-focussed trainees. Download the spreadsheet and note these 8 points weekly.
metadata.og:description: The definitive guide to tracking diet and training progress for serious, physique-focussed trainees. Download the spreadsheet and make sure you are tracking these 8 points of progress weekly. I've re-written and extended the guide explaining the tracking system I have developed and use with clients over the last five years. Hope you find it useful in your quest for shreds. Questions welcome.
metadata.og:title: How To Track Your Physique Progress

data.title: How To Track Body Measurements and Weight for Physique Progress
